title: Carved walnut honey spoon
subtitle: A small decorative spoon carved from a seasoned walnut blank
excerpt: A honey spoon is a small, elegant object: short handle, shallow bowl, the whole thing at a scale that asks for careful thumb-pivot cuts and patience rather than axe work. Walnut is harder than lime or sycamore but produces an extremely fine-grained surface that needs no sanding if the knife is sharp. A useful project for developing precision in small-scale spoon carving.
sourceNotes: Small domestic spoon forms in hardwood documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885).

content.text: A honey spoon is a small object that rewards precision. The whole piece is short enough to be held comfortably in one hand throughout, and the shallow bowl asks for controlled
thumb-pivot cuts

rather than the sustained hook-knife work of a larger bowl. Walnut is harder than lime or sycamore but produces an exceptionally fine-grained surface; a sharp knife leaves it clean without sanding. Starting from a small
seasoned
blank
means there is no axe work and no drying period: the spoon can be carved, oiled, and set aside to cure in a single session.
What you need: wood

A seasoned walnut blank, 18 cm long, 4 cm wide, 12 to 14 mm thick. Walnut blanks at roughly this size are stocked by carving suppliers; the important thing is that the blank is flat, free of checks, and has the grain running along its length. A short piece of off-cut from a larger walnut board works equally well. Cherry at the same dimensions is a good softer alternative. Oak is harder than walnut and the coarser grain makes the finished surface slightly less smooth, but it works.
What you need: tools

A sloyd knife with a very sharp edge; walnut is hard enough that a slightly dull edge causes visible tearing rather than a clean surface. A leather strop, used frequently. A hook knife (medium curve) for the bowl if preferred, though the bowl on a honey spoon is shallow enough to form entirely with thumb-pivot cuts from the sloyd knife. A detail knife for the junction between handle and bowl if the sloyd tip cannot reach cleanly. Sandpaper to 240 grit (optional on a clean knife finish). Raw linseed oil.
The work
Step 1: Mark the profile

Pencil the spoon outline on the wide face of the blank. The handle runs 10 cm and stays at roughly the full width of the blank, with the corners slightly softened. The bowl occupies the front 7 cm and widens to about 3.5 cm at its widest point, then tapers to a rounded tip. Keep the bowl outline simple: a broad oval rather than a pointed form.
Step 2: Cut the outside profile
Use
push cuts
along the sides of the blank to bring the handle and bowl to the pencilled outline. At the rounded bowl tip, use short
thumb-pivot cuts
to work around the curve without slipping past the target line. The blank at this stage still has its full thickness.
Step 3: Thin the bowl from above
With
push cuts

angling from the handle junction toward the tip, thin the bowl section from the front face. The bowl face should thin to about 6 to 7 mm at the deepest point by the time the hollow is started. Keep the handle face flat and at full blank thickness; the spoon back is flat.
Step 4: Form the bowl hollow

The hollow of a honey spoon is shallow: 3 to 4 mm at the deepest point. This can be formed with the sloyd knife tip using thumb-pivot cuts that scoop across the grain, or with a hook knife if available. Work from the centre of the bowl toward the rim in short passes, rotating the spoon to keep the entry angle consistent with the grain. The walls of the bowl at the rim should remain at 4 to 5 mm. A honey spoon bowl does not need to hold a full teaspoon of liquid; it needs enough depth to pick up and release honey cleanly.
Step 5: Round the handle and refine

Round the corners of the handle to an oval or round section using thumb-pivot cuts. The handle end can be left square-cut or rounded into a dome with a few angled push cuts at the tip. Check the whole piece for any flat spots or hard transitions and correct with light passes.
Step 6: Sand (optional)

Walnut that has been cut cleanly with a sharp knife can be oiled directly without sanding. If there are any torn fibres or rough patches, sand through 180 and 240 grit along the grain. Wipe away all dust.
Finishing
Apply raw linseed oil and allow to soak for one hour. Wipe off all surface oil. Cure for two weeks before food contact. Walnut is food-safe once the oil finish has fully cured.
Variations
Carve a set of three in different woods: walnut, cherry, and sycamore. The contrast in finished colour makes them attractive as a set.

Add a small decorative facet to the back face of the handle: three flat push-cut facets running along the length of the handle, stopping before the bowl. A faceted handle is slightly more comfortable in the hand than a fully round one.
Burn a simple line border around the bowl rim with a pyrography tool after oiling, for a decorative inlay effect.
Care
Wipe clean after use and dry. Do not soak. Re-oil every few months. Walnut darkens pleasantly with use and each oiling.

attrs.intro: Problems when carving a small walnut spoon.

items.fix: Strop the knife. Try the cut from the opposite direction. On walnut, a slightly reduced cut angle (flatter bevel against the wood) often produces a cleaner surface.
items.cause: Walnut's grain can reverse in a short distance; the cut is going against it, or the knife is dull.
items.symptom: The wood tears rather than cuts cleanly

items.fix: Mark the centre of the bowl with a pencil dot before you start the hollow. Work equal distances toward it from both sides. Check depth by eye against the pencil mark.
items.cause: Cuts were not taken evenly around both sides of the bowl centre.
items.symptom: The bowl hollow is uneven; one side is deeper than the other

items.fix: Sight along the spoon in profile to see the thickness relationship. Take a few corrective push cuts from the handle toward the junction to slim the transition.
items.cause: Not enough material was removed from the handle to taper it toward the bowl junction.
items.symptom: The handle looks chunky relative to the bowl

attrs.heading: Common problems
